questions.question: Which big cat can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h faster than most sports cars — going from a standing start to highway speed in just three seconds?
questions.source_excerpt: The cheetah can accelerate from 0 to 96.6 km/h (60.0 mph) in under three seconds.

questions.question: Sloths move so slowly that something amazing actually grows in their fur — what is it?
questions.source_excerpt: Sloths' fur exhibits specialized functions: it provides camouflage and is host to symbiotic algae.

questions.question: Whether a baby crocodile hatches as a boy or a girl is decided by something kids can easily measure with a thermometer — what is it?
questions.source_excerpt: In crocodilians, all eggs incubated below approximately 31 °C produce females; at intermediate temperatures, males predominate.

questions.question: Polar bears look snow-white, but if you could shave one (please don't!) you'd find their skin underneath is a very surprising color. What color is it?
questions.source_excerpt: Beneath the polar bear's fur, the skin is black; the hairs are colorless and hollow.

questions.question: What do you call a group of crows when they're all hanging out together — a flock, a herd, or a murder?
questions.source_excerpt: A group of crows is known as a murder.

questions.question: If you weighed everything in the solar system on a giant scale, the Sun by itself would account for what surprising share of the total — about half, about three-quarters, or over 99 percent?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): Sun is ~99.86% of solar system mass — confirmed by mccc.edu, IFLScience, space.com, and multiple sources. Answer 'Over 99 percent' agrees.
questions.source_excerpt: The Sun accounts for about 99.86% of the total mass of the Solar System.

questions.question: Astronauts come back from a few months on the space station having grown a few centimeters TALLER. Why does this happen?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): Scientific American + Space.com confirm spinal elongation in microgravity, up to ~5 cm. Answer 'no gravity stretches their spine' is the standard physiological explanation.
questions.source_excerpt: Astronauts may experience a height increase of up to 5 cm during long-duration spaceflight as the spine decompresses.

questions.question: True or false: a single day on the planet Venus is actually LONGER than a whole year on Venus.
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): Snopes 'True' rating + space.com + spaceplace.nasa.gov confirm Venus rotation 243d, orbit 225d. Answer 'True' is correct.
questions.source_excerpt: A sidereal day on Venus lasts 243.0226 Earth days, longer than its orbital period of 224.7 Earth days.

questions.question: We're born with about 300 of one body part, but by the time we're grown up, we only have 206. Some of them fuse together! What body part is it?
questions.source_excerpt: An adult human has 206 bones, while a newborn baby has roughly 300 because some bones fuse during growth.

questions.question: Just like fingerprints, every person on Earth has a totally unique pattern on this part of their body — the part you stick out when you make a silly face!
questions.source_excerpt: The pattern of papillae and texture of the tongue is considered unique to each individual.

questions.question: The Moon is slowly drifting away from Earth — about how much further away does it get every year: a millimeter, a few centimeters, or a meter?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): IFLScience, BBC, NASA-cited NRAO confirm 3.8 cm/yr lunar recession measured via Apollo retroreflectors. Answer 'a few centimeters' is correct.
questions.source_excerpt: Lunar laser ranging has shown that the Moon recedes from Earth at a rate of approximately 3.8 cm per year.

questions.question: An engineer was walking his dog and noticed something prickly clinging to its fur. He looked at it under a microscope and it inspired one of the most useful sticky inventions ever — what did he invent?
questions.source_excerpt: George de Mestral invented Velcro after observing how burrs of the burdock plant clung to his dog's fur.

questions.question: Carrots haven't always been orange. The first cultivated carrots, hundreds of years ago, were a totally different color — what was it?
questions.source_excerpt: The cultivated carrot probably originated in Persia in shades of purple and yellow; the orange carrot was developed later in the Netherlands.

questions.question: If the world's smallest country is the Vatican, which country is the BIGGEST in pure land area?
questions.source_excerpt: Russia is the largest country in the world, covering about 17,098,242 km².

questions.question: Honeybees can do something that scientists thought only smart mammals could do — they can recognize and remember individual ones of these. What?
questions.source_excerpt: Studies have demonstrated that honey bees can be trained to recognize human faces, displaying configural processing previously thought to require larger brains.

questions.question: Microwave ovens were invented by complete accident — when an engineer noticed something in his pocket suddenly melted near a radar machine. What was in his pocket?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): MIT Technology Review + Raytheon corp lore + multiple history sources confirm Percy Spencer's 1945 melted chocolate bar discovery. Answer 'a chocolate bar' is correct.
questions.source_excerpt: Engineer Percy Spencer accidentally discovered the heating effect when a chocolate bar in his pocket melted while standing near an active magnetron.

questions.question: If you could fold a normal sheet of paper in half 42 times, surprisingly, how thick would it become — taller than a house, taller than a mountain, or all the way to the Moon?
questions.stored_answer: All the way to the Moon
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): codersrevolution + boundlessbrilliance + medium 'Starts With a Bang' confirm exponential math: 0.1mm × 2^42 ≈ 440,000 km > 384,400 km Earth-Moon distance. Question explicitly notes paper rips physically — math is the point.
questions.source_excerpt: Doubling 0.1 mm of paper 42 times yields more than 4×10^11 mm, roughly the average Earth–Moon distance.

questions.question: Octopuses are surprisingly clever — they can even open which everyday human object that has a screwed-on lid?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): Scientific American 'Speedy Octopus Sets Record for Jar-Opening' + The Dodo confirm octopuses unscrew jar lids. Answer 'a jar' is correct.
questions.source_excerpt: Captive octopuses are well known for their problem-solving abilities, including unscrewing the lids of jars.

questions.question: Some sea turtles alive today belong to a family that's older than something else amazing — they were swimming around BEFORE which famous extinct creatures appeared?
questions.source_excerpt: Turtles are among the oldest reptile groups, with the earliest known fossils dating from about 230 million years ago, predating most dinosaurs.

questions.question: Mount Everest is so tall it's still growing — about how much taller does it get every year: a millimeter, a few millimeters, or a meter?
questions.notes: Claude override (Gemini outage): UCL 2024 study + BBC + Smithsonian discuss isostatic-rebound contribution; long-term tectonic uplift ~4mm/yr is widely cited (recent measurements vary 0.5–4mm). Among the offered options (a millimeter / a few millimeters / a meter), 'a few millimeters' is the correct order-of-magnitude bucket.
questions.source_excerpt: Mount Everest is rising by approximately 4 mm each year due to the ongoing collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.
